alsoKnownAs: Hong Kong Plec (misnomer — not a plecostomus)

nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated mountain streams and rivers with rocky substrates; exposed to strong current over smooth boulders and cobble covered in biofilm and diatom mats (aufwuchs)

decorPreferences: smooth river stones and large flat rocks for resting and foraging
cobble and pebble substrate allowing biofilm growth
powerful circulation pump or powerhead to simulate torrent conditions
mature tank with established algae/diatom/biofilm growth on rocks
low-profile decorations to preserve flow lanes
fine sand or gravel between rocks for occasional substrate resting

diet.notes: In the wild, the Butterfly Hillstream Loach is a specialised grazer of aufwuchs — the thin layer of biofilm, diatoms, micro-algae, and associated micro-organisms that coats rock surfaces in fast-flowing streams. In captivity, natural algae and biofilm growth on rocks must be abundant and is the foundation of the diet. This can be supplemented with spirulina wafers, algae tabs pressed against the glass, blanched zucchini or cucumber, and high-quality sinking micro-pellets. Frozen or live micro-foods such as daphnia or baby brine shrimp are accepted as an occasional addition. The species frequently starves in low-flow tanks where insufficient aufwuchs develops, so strong circulation and allowing algae to establish naturally on rocks are essential.

goodWith: other hillstream loaches (Sewellia, Gastromyzon — if same temperature range)
White Cloud Mountain Minnows (same cool-water preference)
small danios (Celestial Pearl Danio, Zebra Danio) in cool setups
dojo loach (Misgurnus) — if tank is large enough
avoidWith: tropical community fish requiring 26°C+ (Neon Tetras, Discus, Angels, Guppies — dangerously warm for this species)
large boisterous fish that outcompete for food
fish requiring still or low-flow water
Sewellia lineolata (Reticulated Hillstream Loach) — different range and care requirements, commonly misidentified as this species

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Beaufortia kweichowensis — verify availability; typical FishBase contributed photo.
Wikimedia Commons image of Beaufortia kweichowensis — confirm URL resolves; file name is approximate.

summary: The Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis) is a flattened, ray-shaped freshwater fish from fast-flowing mountain streams in China, adapted for strong current with adhesive fins. It requires cool (18–24°C), highly oxygenated, high-flow water and mature biofilm-covered rocks and is unsuitable for warm community tanks.
careNotes: Beaufortia kweichowensis is an advanced-care species that fails to thrive — and commonly dies slowly — when placed in standard tropical community tanks. The fundamental requirement is cool, oxygen-saturated, high-flow water mimicking the rocky torrent habitats of Guizhou Province, China. Temperature must be kept at 18–24°C; sustained temperatures above 25°C cause oxygen depletion stress and gradual decline. A powerful circulation pump or multiple powerheads should create near-continuous strong current across flat rock surfaces, which is where the fish will spend most of its time. A minimum tank size of 80 L (75 cm footprint) is recommended for a small group, but wider tanks with more rock surface area are strongly preferred. The fish uses modified pectoral and pelvic fins as adhesive pads to cling to rocks against the current — décor must include broad, flat smooth stones that can be colonised by diatoms and biofilm. The single most common cause of loss is starvation in tanks where aufwuchs growth is insufficient. This fish is not an algae-eater in the sense of consuming visible green algae sheets; it specialises in scraping the thin microbial-diatom layer (aufwuchs) that develops on rock surfaces in well-lit, high-flow conditions. New tanks or tanks with inadequate flow rarely develop this food source. Supplement generously with spirulina wafers, algae tabs pressed to the glass, and blanched vegetables. The trade name 'Hong Kong Plec' is a persistent misnomer; this species is entirely unrelated to plecos (Loricariidae) and has completely different care requirements. It should not be confused with Sewellia lineolata (Reticulated Hillstream Loach) from Vietnam, which has a distinct reticulated pattern and is a separate genus. Water quality must be high — this species is sensitive to dissolved organics, elevated nitrates, and low oxygen; frequent partial water changes and efficient mechanical filtration are essential. Males can develop small tubercles on the snout; slight territorial squabbling between males is normal.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is reported but uncommon. Males develop nuptial tubercles (small bumps) on the snout and head. Eggs are deposited in crevices or under rocks in high-flow areas and are guarded loosely by the male. Fry are tiny and require abundant micro-aufwuchs or cultured micro-algae. Precise conditioning with a temperature dip (simulating seasonal cooling), increased flow, and a varied diet rich in spirulina may trigger spawning. Success is considerably easier in species-only hillstream setups where biofilm is consistently available and competition is absent.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Beaufortia kweichowensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Detailed species profile including taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, formerly Balitoridae), native range (Guizhou/Guangdong provinces, China), water parameters (18–23°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 36–179 ppm), adult size (~8 cm), habitat (fast-flowing rocky streams), diet (aufwuchs), care requirements, and breeding behaviour.

additional.name: FishBase — Beaufortia kweichowensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (max ~8 cm TL), native range (China: Pearl River drainage, Guizhou), temperature range, and habitat notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hillstream Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide covering Beaufortia kweichowensis and related hillstream loaches. Confirmed high-flow requirement, cool temperature (68–75°F / 20–24°C), aufwuchs diet, tank size and décor recommendations, and common failure modes (starvation in low-flow tanks, overheating in tropical community tanks).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hillstream Loaches
additional.notes: Feature article covering hillstream loach genera including Beaufortia. Confirmed misidentification issues with Sewellia lineolata, biofilm/aufwuchs grazing specialisation, oxygenation requirements, and cool-water temperature demands. Notes misnomer 'Hong Kong Plec' and incorrect placement into tropical community tanks as primary cause of mortality.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Hillstream Loaches: The Torrent Surfers
additional.notes: Specialist article on hillstream loach natural history and aquarium husbandry. Confirmed fast-water torrent biotope, Gastromyzontidae family placement, flat body morphology as torrent adaptation, and dietary specialisation for micro-aufwuchs over macroalgae.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are deposited in rock crevices under high-flow conditions, and males develop nuptial tubercles (small bumps on the snout and head) during breeding condition. Fry are tiny and require an established supply of micro-aufwuchs or cultured micro-algae. A seasonal temperature dip combined with increased flow may trigger spawning. Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved, but success is considerably more likely in a species-only hillstream biotope setup where biofilm is consistently available.
